draft.introduction: The Mahāsatipaṭṭhāna Sutta is the foundational text for Buddhist meditation practice. It describes the four foundations of mindfulness: body, feelings, mind, and dhammas.

translation.text: The Blessed One addressed the monks: “This is the direct path for the purification of beings, for the surmounting of sorrow, for the disappearance of suffering, for the attainment of the true way, for the realization of Nibbāna—namely, the four foundations of mindfulness.”
translation.original_text: evaṃ me sutaṃ | ekaṃ samayaṃ bhagavā kurusu viharati

translation.text: And how does a monk practice contemplating the body as body? Here a monk, having gone to the forest or to an empty hut, sits down cross-legged, holds his body erect, and establishes mindfulness. Mindfully he breathes in, mindfully he breathes out.
translation.original_text: kathā ca bhikkhave bhikkhu kāye kāyānupassī viharati

translator_notes.note: The phrase “kāye kāyānupassī” (“contemplating the body as body”) uses an unusual reduplicative construction meaning: seeing the body only as body—not as “my body,” not as beautiful or ugly, but simply as body. This is mindfulness stripped of conceptual overlay.
